musicalLanguage.description: George Gershwin's Summertime runs 48 measures at a gentle 72 BPM, and its structure is built on a remarkably exact large-scale echo: five separate recurring figures, independently found by the pattern pass, each show an identical gap of exactly 18 measures between their two occurrences — for example a 43-note left-hand passage at measures 13-17 recurs at measures 31-35, and a right-hand figure at measures 15-16 recurs at measures 33-34. The piece's busiest measures confirm this independently: measures 16 and 34 are exactly tied as the busiest in the piece (25 onsets each), and measures 15 and 33 are both near the top of the list too — every one of these pairs sits exactly 18 measures apart. Harmonically, only 3 chord windows clear the confidence gate (6% of windows considered, all hedged as inferred) — but E dominant seventh, appearing twice, is the textbook dominant seventh of the stated and confirmed A minor tonic, a safe, functional reading even from such thin data.
musicalLanguage.structure: The piece opens with the left hand resting for 3 measures and the right hand for 2 (measures 1-3), the longest rest gaps of either hand, before the section lens's largest novelty spike (8.398) opens the main material at measure 5. That material states once across roughly measures 13-22 and restates, confirmed by five independent pattern matches, exactly 18 measures later across roughly measures 31-40, an unusually exact structural echo for a piece this length. A second, smaller novelty spike (5.4) opens the final section at measure 44, just before the piece thins toward its close.
keyMoments: Five separate recurring figures, independently found by the pattern pass, each show an identical gap of exactly 18 measures between their two occurrences: a left-hand passage at measures 13-17 and 31-35, right-hand figures at measures 15-16/33-34, 17-18/35-36, and 21-22/39-40, and a left-hand figure at measures 20-22/38-40 — this consistent spacing is strong, multiply-corroborated evidence of a genuine large-scale structural repeat.

Measures 16 and 34 are exactly tied as the busiest in the piece (25 onsets each) and sit exactly 18 measures apart, independently confirming the same structural echo the pattern pass finds — the piece's density peaks and its large-scale repetition are the same event.

E dominant seventh appears twice among just 3 total confidence-gated windows, a thin sample, but a musically safe one: it is the textbook dominant seventh of the confirmed A minor tonic (statedKeyFit 0.493, matching the content-detected key exactly).

The piece opens with the left hand resting 3 measures and the right hand 2 (measures 1-3), the longest rest gaps of either hand, establishing a genuinely quiet entrance before the section lens's largest novelty spike (8.398) opens the main material at measure 5.
teachingGoals: Learn measures 13-22 as the core material of the piece — the pattern pass confirms, via five independent matches, that measures 31-40 restate it exactly 18 measures later.

Practice recognizing E dominant seventh as the one safely confirmed harmonic color in the piece, the dominant seventh of the confirmed A minor tonic, without expecting more from the thin remaining chord data.
Build a genuinely quiet, controlled entrance for the first few measures — both hands rest longer here (3 measures left hand, 2 right hand) than they do at any other point in the piece.

Master measures 16 and 34 together as a linked pair, since both are exactly tied as the piece's busiest measures and sit exactly 18 measures apart, the same structural echo confirmed two different ways.
styleTips: Play the opening measures with real stillness, letting the left hand's 3-measure rest and the right hand's 2-measure rest set a genuinely hushed mood before the melody enters at measure 5.

Pedal generously through the E dominant seventh at its two appearances, giving the resolution back toward A minor room to breathe rather than rushing through the only clearly confirmed harmonic color in the piece.

Listen for the return of the measures 13-22 material at measures 31-40 as a recognizable second verse, letting your touch acknowledge the exact 18-measure echo rather than playing it as brand-new material.

Try keeping the tempo unhurried throughout — at 72 BPM this is a genuine lullaby-paced ballad, and the exact structural repetition rewards a performance patient enough to let the listener recognize it.
